skill.name: azure-resource-manager-mysql-dotnet
skill.description: Guidance for using the Azure.ResourceManager.MySql .NET SDK to manage Azure Database for MySQL Flexible Server resources.
skill.summary: Manage Azure MySQL Flexible Server resources with the .NET Azure Resource Manager SDK.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, and blocker patterns, but most are Markdown examples or Azure documentation references rather than executable skill behavior. The skill is documentation-only, but publication should carry a warning because examples include hardcoded credential placeholders, broad Azure service firewall access, and destructive resource operations.

medium_findings.title: Hardcoded Credential Placeholder in Examples
medium_findings.description: The C# samples place an administrator password and connection string password directly in code. This appears to be instructional sample text, but users could copy it into real projects and expose database credentials.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The password values are visible in concrete code examples. The context is documentation, so the risk is user copy-paste misuse rather than malicious execution by the skill.

medium_findings.title: Broad Azure Services Firewall Rule
medium_findings.description: The firewall example uses the Azure special 0.0.0.0 rule to allow Azure services. This is a legitimate Azure pattern, but it can broaden database access if users apply it without network review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly creates an Azure services firewall rule using 0.0.0.0. The pattern is documented by Azure, but it has real exposure implications.

medium_findings.title: Destructive Resource Operations Shown Without Guardrails
medium_findings.description: The workflow includes stop, restart, failover, scale, and delete operations for database servers. These are expected management tasks, but examples lack confirmation checks or environment safeguards.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The operations are explicit Azure management calls with production impact. They are not malicious, but careless use can cause downtime or data loss.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misidentified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The repeated Ruby or shell backtick findings are caused by Markdown fenced code blocks and inline code spans. They are not executable skill scripts and do not run automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only file is SKILL.md and the flagged regions are Markdown examples or tables. No script file, hook, or executable wrapper is present.

low_findings.title: Filesystem and Blocker Patterns Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The path traversal, hidden file, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance alerts point to Azure resource identifiers, OAuth scope text, high availability labels, or error logging examples. No local filesystem traversal, weak crypto use, or host reconnaissance is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review shows these strings are Azure documentation constructs. They do not invoke filesystem APIs, cryptographic algorithms, or system inventory commands.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Reference Links
low_findings.description: The URL findings are links to NuGet, Microsoft Learn, Azure product documentation, and Azure SDK source. No evidence found of data exfiltration or unauthorized network calls.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in a Reference Links table and are official or expected package documentation destinations. They are not called by code in the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Copy-Paste Credential Risk
dangerous_patterns.description: Password placeholders are embedded inside runnable-looking C# samples. Users should replace them with secret storage or managed identity patterns before production use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The samples contain literal password strings in credential fields. The risk is credible if users copy the examples into source control.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad Firewall Access Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The sample allows Azure services with the 0.0.0.0 firewall rule. Teams should prefer private endpoints or narrow rules when possible.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The firewall rule is directly shown and has a known network access impact. It remains a legitimate administrative option in some environments.

dangerous_patterns.title: Destructive Cloud Management Calls
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples include stop, restart, update, failover, and delete calls against MySQL Flexible Server resources. These calls require careful subscription and environment targeting.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The operations are plainly visible as Azure SDK calls. The content is instructional, so the risk depends on how users apply the examples.

content.value_statement: Managing Azure MySQL Flexible Server requires many SDK types and operational choices. This skill gives focused .NET Azure Resource Manager guidance for common database administration workflows.
actual_capabilities: Shows package installation commands for Azure.ResourceManager.MySql and Azure.Identity.
Explains authentication with ArmClient and DefaultAzureCredential.
Provides examples for creating MySQL Flexible Server instances and databases.
Covers firewall rules, server configuration, Entra ID administrators, and backups.
Demonstrates stop, start, restart, scale, and delete management workflows.
Links to NuGet, Microsoft Learn API references, product documentation, and SDK source.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not execute Azure operations by itself.
Examples use placeholders that must be replaced with secure production values.
It focuses on Flexible Server and does not cover retired Single Server workflows.
It does not include full application architecture or migration planning guidance.

use_cases.title: Provision a database environment
use_cases.description: Create a MySQL Flexible Server, database, storage settings, backup policy, and high availability configuration from .NET code.

use_cases.title: Automate operational changes
use_cases.description: Use Azure SDK examples to adjust firewall rules, tune server parameters, scale resources, or restart a server.

use_cases.title: Review SDK object models
use_cases.description: Map Azure MySQL management tasks to SDK resource, collection, data, and model types.

prompt_templates.title: Create a starter server sample
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to draft a basic .NET sample that creates an Azure MySQL Flexible Server with safe placeholders.

prompt_templates.title: Add firewall and database workflows
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to add database creation and narrow firewall rule management to my Azure MySQL .NET automation.

prompt_templates.title: Harden production configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Azure MySQL Flexible Server settings for backup retention, high availability, private access, and credential handling.

prompt_templates.title: Design an operations runbook
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create a .NET runbook for scaling, restarting, restoring, and safely deleting Azure MySQL resources.

output_examples.input: Create a MySQL Flexible Server with the Azure SDK for .NET.
output_examples.output: A guided implementation outline with required packages, authentication steps, server settings, storage options, backup policy, and high availability choices.

output_examples.input: Review my firewall rule plan for Azure MySQL.
output_examples.output: A security-focused review that compares narrow IP ranges, Azure service access, and private endpoint recommendations.

output_examples.input: Prepare a restore workflow for an Azure MySQL server.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step plan for finding backups, selecting a restore point, creating a restored server, and validating application connectivity.

best_practices: Use DefaultAzureCredential or managed identity instead of embedded passwords.
Prefer private endpoints and narrow firewall rules for production databases.
Test destructive operations in nonproduction subscriptions before using them in production.
anti_patterns: Do not copy placeholder passwords into source code or deployment scripts.
Do not enable broad Azure service access without network approval.
Do not run delete, restart, or failover workflows without environment checks.

faq.question: Does this skill run Azure commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides documentation and examples for using the Azure SDK for .NET.

faq.question: Which Azure database product does it cover?
faq.answer: It focuses on Azure Database for MySQL Flexible Server.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it include secure authentication guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows DefaultAzureCredential and recommends Entra ID token-based access.

faq.question: What should I change before production use?
faq.answer: Replace password placeholders, narrow firewall rules, confirm backup settings, and validate subscription targets.

faq.question: Does it cover Azure MySQL Single Server?
faq.answer: No. The skill states that Single Server is deprecated and focuses on Flexible Server.
